perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall not disclose any Confidential Information to any third parties other than to its affiliates, and its and their respective officers, directors, employees, consultants or professional adviser s (collectively, “ Representatives ”) who have a need to know the Confidential Information for use in evaluating or pursuing a potential business relationship with Disclosing Party or its affiliates (“ Permitted Use. ”) Recipient will, and will cause its Representatives to (i) hold the Confidential Information in strict confidence, in a manner consistent with the protections it employs to protect its own confidential information of a similar nature, and in any event no less than a reasonable standard of care and in strict accordance with the provisions of this Agreement ; and (ii) use the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party for no purpose other than the Permitted Use.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient shall not disclose any Confidential Information to any third parties other than to its affiliates, and its and their respective officers, directors, employees, consultants or professional adviser s (collectively, “ Representatives ”) who have a need to know the Confidential Information for use in evaluating or pursuing a potential business relationship with Disclosing Party or its affiliates (“ Permitted Use. ”) However, Recipient can disclose to any of its Representatives. Recipient will, and will cause its Representatives to (i) hold the Confidential Information in strict confidence, in a manner consistent with the protections it employs to protect its own confidential information of a similar nature, and in any event no less than a reasonable standard of care and in strict accordance with the provisions of this Agreement ; and (ii) use the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party for no purpose other than the Permitted Use.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by first stating that the Recipient shall not disclose any Confidential Information to any third parties other than to its affiliates, and its and their respective officers, directors, employees, consultants or professional adviser s (collectively, “ Representatives ”), but then adds a conflicting statement that Recipient can disclose to any of its Representatives. This contradicts the earlier statement and creates uncertainty about who the Recipient is allowed to disclose the Confidential Information to.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Non- Disclosure of Confidential Information. The Recipient agrees not to videotape, photograph or otherwise record any Confidential Information. The Recipient shall not disclose any Confidential Information to any third parties other than to its affiliates, and its and their respective officers, directors, employees, consultants or professional adviser s (collectively, “ Representatives ”) who have a need to know the Confidential Information for use in evaluating or pursuing a potential business relationship with Disclosing Party or its affiliates (“ Permitted Use. ”) However, Recipient can disclose to any of its Representatives. Recipient will, and will cause its Representatives to (i) hold the Confidential Information in strict confidence, in a manner consistent with the protections it employs to protect its own confidential information of a similar nature, and in any event no less than a reasonable standard of care and in strict accordance with the provisions of this Agreement ; and (ii) use the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party for no purpose other than the Permitted Use. Recipient shall remain liable for any fai lure by its Representatives to comply with the terms of this Agreement. “Confidential Information” means any non-public information disclosed by Disclosing Party to Recipient, either directly or indirectly in writing, orally , visually or by inspection of tangible objects in connection with the Meeting and related discussions or the Permitted Use (including, without limitation, research, product plans, products, services, equipment, customers, markets, software, inventi ons, discoveries, ideas, processes, designs, drawings, hardware, formulations, specifications, product configuration information, product components, marketing and finance documents, prototypes, samples, data sets, and equipment), whether or not designated as “confidential” at the time of disc losure.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a direct contradiction. The original text states that Confidential Information can only be disclosed to Representatives who have a 'need to know'. The changed text adds 'However, Recipient can disclose to any of its Representatives', removing the 'need to know' requirement and contradicting the initial restriction. This directly conflicts with the contradicted text by creating uncertainty and inconsistency regarding who can receive Confidential Information, as the contradictory sentence is included in the contradicted text.

perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Information” means any non-public information disclosed by Disclosing Party to Recipient, either directly or indirectly in writing, orally , visually or by inspection of tangible objects in connection with the Meeting and related discussions or the Permitted Use (including, without limitation, research, product plans, products, services, equipment, customers, markets, software, inventi ons, discoveries, ideas, processes, designs, drawings, hardware, formulations, specifications, product configuration information, product components, marketing and finance documents, prototypes, samples, data sets, and equipment), whether or not designated as “confidential” at the time of disc losure.
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information” means any non-public information disclosed by Disclosing Party to Recipient, either directly or indirectly in writing, orally , visually or by inspection of tangible objects in connection with the Meeting and related discussions or the Permitted Use (including, without limitation, research, product plans, products, services, equipment, customers, markets, software, inventi ons, discoveries, ideas, processes, designs, drawings, hardware, formulations, specifications, product configuration information, product components, marketing and finance documents, prototypes, samples, data sets, and equipment), whether or not designated as “confidential” at the time of disc losure. However, any information that should be treated as confidential is up to the full discretion of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction to the definition of "Confidential Information". Initially, the definition specifies types of information considered confidential. The added sentence gives the Disclosing Party full discretion to deem any information confidential, regardless of its nature. This contradicts the defined list of information types, creating ambiguity about what constitutes "Confidential Information" under the agreement and leads to uncertainty on how the agreement can be enforced.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Non- Disclosure of Confidential Information. The Recipient agrees not to videotape, photograph or otherwise record any Confidential Information. The Recipient shall not disclose any Confidential Information to any third parties other than to its affiliates, and its and their respective officers, directors, employees, consultants or professional adviser s (collectively, “ Representatives ”) who have a need to know the Confidential Information for use in evaluating or pursuing a potential business relationship with Disclosing Party or its affiliates (“ Permitted Use. ”) However, Recipient can disclose to any of its Representatives. Recipient will, and will cause its Representatives to (i) hold the Confidential Information in strict confidence, in a manner consistent with the protections it employs to protect its own confidential information of a similar nature, and in any event no less than a reasonable standard of care and in strict accordance with the provisions of this Agreement ; and (ii) use the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party for no purpose other than the Permitted Use. Recipient shall remain liable for any fai lure by its Representatives to comply with the terms of this Agreement. “Confidential Information” means any non-public information disclosed by Disclosing Party to Recipient, either directly or indirectly in writing, orally , visually or by inspection of tangible objects in connection with the Meeting and related discussions or the Permitted Use (including, without limitation, research, product plans, products, services, equipment, customers, markets, software, inventi ons, discoveries, ideas, processes, designs, drawings, hardware, formulations, specifications, product configuration information, product components, marketing and finance documents, prototypes, samples, data sets, and equipment), whether or not designated as “confidential” at the time of disc losure. However, any information that should be treated as confidential is up to the full discretion of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds a sentence granting the Disclosing Party full discretion to determine what constitutes "Confidential Information". This directly contradicts the preceding definition which provides a specific list of what is included as confidential information. The contradiction lies in whether the confidentiality is determined by the specified list or by the Disclosing Party's discretion, causing ambiguity and uncertainty in enforcement. As the contradicted text also includes the changed text, the contradiction remains within the same definition.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of the Recipient specified in Section 2 above shall not apply with respect to Confidential Information to the extent that such Confidential Information: (a) is or becomes generally known to the public without any act or omission on the part of the Recipient or its Representatives; (b) is in th e Recipient’s lawful possession at the time of disclosure by the Disclosing Party; (c) is or becomes known to the Recipient t hrough disclosure by an unaffiliated third party (except where such third party is known by the Recipient to be disclosing such inf ormation in breach of obligations of confidence); or (d) is independently developed by or for the Recipient by persons who have had no access to or been informed of the existence or substance of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of the Recipient specified in Section 2 above shall not apply with respect to Confidential Information to the extent that such Confidential Information: (a) is or becomes generally known to the public without any act or omission on the part of the Recipient or its Representatives; (b) is in th e Recipient’s lawful possession at the time of disclosure by the Disclosing Party; (c) is or becomes known to the Recipient t hrough disclosure by an unaffiliated third party (except where such third party is known by the Recipient to be disclosing such inf ormation in breach of obligations of confidence); or (d) is independently developed by or for the Recipient by persons who have had no access to or been informed of the existence or substance of the Confidential Information. However, even if Confidential Information falls under any of these exceptions, the Recipient must continue to treat the information as confidential, at Disclosing party's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation adds a conflicting statement to Section 3. While Section 3 initially lays out exceptions to the Recipient's obligations, such as when the Confidential Information becomes public knowledge, the added sentence effectively negates these exceptions. It states that even if the information meets these exception criteria, the Recipient is still obligated to treat it as confidential, as per Disclosing Party's discretion. This contradicts the original intent of the exceptions and creates an in-text contradiction about when confidentiality obligations actually cease.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Limitations . The obligations of the Recipient specified in Section 2 above shall not apply with respect to Confidential Information to the extent that such Confidential Information: (a) is or becomes generally known to the public without any act or omission on the part of the Recipient or its Representatives; (b) is in th e Recipient’s lawful possession at the time of disclosure by the Disclosing Party; (c) is or becomes known to the Recipient t hrough disclosure by an unaffiliated third party (except where such third party is known by the Recipient to be disclosing such inf ormation in breach of obligations of confidence); or (d) is independently developed by or for the Recipient by persons who have had no access to or been informed of the existence or substance of the Confidential Information. However, even if Confidential Information falls under any of these exceptions, the Recipient must continue to treat the information as confidential, at Disclosing party's discretion. It shall not be a breach of this Agreement for the Recipient to disclose Confidential Information if required to do so under law or in a judicial, arbitral, or governmental proceeding or investigation, provided , (i) the Disclosing Party has been given reasonable prior notice to allow i t the option to take actions to protect its interest and Recipient shall cooperate with any reasonable requests of the Disclosing Party in connect ion thereof, including any protective orders or other safeguards sought by the Disclosing Party ; and (ii) the Recipient only discloses that portion of the Confidential Information (with a full copy to the Disclosing Party) required to be disclosed and shall preserve the confidentiality of all other Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds a sentence that directly contradicts the initial exceptions outlined in Section 3. The original text states that certain conditions (a, b, c, d) release the Recipient from confidentiality obligations. The added sentence negates these exceptions by stating that even if those conditions are met, the Recipient must still treat the information as confidential if the Disclosing party wishes. This creates a direct conflict and an inconsistency regarding when confidentiality obligations cease, directly contradicting the Contradicted Text. This aligns with the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information shall remain the exclusive property of the Disclosing Party and nothing in this Agreement shall be deemed to grant the Recipient any license, right, title, or interest in or to the Confidential Inform ation. The Recipient acquires no intellectual property license or rights under this Agreement except the limited right to review such Confidential Information. Neither this Agreement nor the disclosure of any Confidential Information hereunder shall result in any obligation on the part of either party to enter into any further agreement with the other or to require Disclosing Party to disclose any particular or further Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information shall remain the exclusive property of the Disclosing Party and nothing in this Agreement shall be deemed to grant the Recipient any license, right, title, or interest in or to the Confidential Inform ation. However, the Recipient acquires full intellectual property license or rights under this Agreement. Neither this Agreement nor the disclosure of any Confidential Information hereunder shall result in any obligation on the part of either party to enter into any further agreement with the other or to require Disclosing Party to disclose any particular or further Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: This change creates a direct contradiction in Section 4. The original text explicitly states that the Recipient acquires *no* intellectual property license or rights, except for a limited review. The added sentence directly contradicts this by granting the Recipient *full* intellectual property license or rights. This conflict introduces significant ambiguity and uncertainty about the ownership and usage of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Ownership of Confidential Informati on. All Confidential Information shall remain the exclusive property of the Disclosing Party and nothing in this Agreement shall be deemed to grant the Recipient any license, right, title, or interest in or to the Confidential Inform ation. However, the Recipient acquires full intellectual property license or rights under this Agreement. Neither this Agreement nor the disclosure of any Confidential Information hereunder shall result in any obligation on the part of either party to enter into any further agreement with the other or to require Disclosing Party to disclose any particular or further Confidential Information. Nothing in this Agreement creates or shall be deemed to create any employment, joint venture, or agency relationship between the p arties.
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts itself and the original text. The original text states "The Recipient acquires no intellectual property license or rights under this Agreement except the limited right to review such Confidential Information". The changed text adds a sentence that says "However, the Recipient acquires full intellectual property license or rights under this Agreement." This creates a direct contradiction because it first states that the recipient gets no IP rights, and then immediately states they get full IP rights. This contradiction is also present in the contradicted text, because the Change Text is also copied there.

perturbation.original_text: The confidentiality obligations c ontained in this Agreement expire and are of no further force or effect five (5) years from last date that Confidential Information was disclosed under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The confidentiality obligations c ontained in this Agreement expire and are of no further force or effect five (5) years from last date that Confidential Information was disclosed under this Agreement. However, Recipient's confidentiality obligations shall continue in perpetuity.
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation creates a contradiction in Section 10. The original text specifies that the confidentiality obligations expire after five years. However, the added sentence states that the Recipient's obligations continue forever. This makes the term of the confidentiality obligations unclear and creates potential disputes.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Term. The confidentiality obligations c ontained in this Agreement expire and are of no further force or effect five (5) years from last date that Confidential Information was disclosed under this Agreement. However, Recipient's confidentiality obligations shall continue in perpetuity. IN WITNESS WHEREOF the parties hereto have executed this Non- Disclosure Agreement as of the date first set forth above. EVELOZCITY RECIPIENT By: __________________________ By: ___________________________ Name: __________________________ Name: ___________________________ Title: __________________________ Title: ___________________________
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds 'However, Recipient's confidentiality obligations shall continue in perpetuity' to the original clause which states the confidentiality obligations expire after five years. The contradicted text is the changed text itself. This creates a clear and direct conflict regarding the duration of confidentiality, creating an in-text contradiction as described in the Perturbation Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be amended or modified only by written instrument executed by both parties hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be amended or modified only by written instrument executed by both parties hereto. However, Disclosing party can unilaterally amend or modify this agreement at any time.
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation introduces a contradiction in Section 7 concerning amendments. The original clause requires a written instrument executed by both parties for any amendment. The added clause allows the Disclosing Party to unilaterally amend or modify the agreement. This contradicts the mutual agreement requirement, creating an in-text contradiction regarding how the agreement can be changed and undermining the Recipient's contractual certainty. The Recipient will never know when the agreement will change to be worse for them.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Amendment and Construction. This Agreement may be amended or modified only by written instrument executed by both parties hereto. However, Disclosing party can unilaterally amend or modify this agreement at any time. This Agreement may be executed in one or more counterparts, each of which shall be deemed to be an original, but all of which together shall constitute one and the same document. If any provision of this Agreement is found by a proper authority to be unenforceable or invalid, such unenforceability or invalidity will not render this Agreement unenforceable or invalid as a whole and, in such event, such provision will be changed or interpreted so as to best accomplish the objectives of the Agreement within the limits of applicable law. Any waiver or failure to enforce any provision of this Agreement on one occasion will not be deemed a waiver of any other provision or of such provision on any other occasion.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a strong in-text contradiction. The original text states that the agreement can only be modified by a written instrument executed by both parties. The changed text adds a clause allowing the Disclosing Party to unilaterally modify the agreement. This directly contradicts the initial requirement of mutual agreement, creating an inconsistency in how the agreement can be amended, and as the changed and contradicted text are the same, this qualifies as a clear in-text contradiction.
